subject: Physics
unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: What is centripetal force?

options.A: A force directed away from the center
options.B: A force directed toward the center
options.C: A force tangent to the circle
options.D: A force that changes the speed of an object

questions.explanation: Centripetal force is the net force causing centripetal acceleration, always directed toward the center of the circular path. Option A describes centrifugal force (fictitious), not centripetal.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.content_anchor: force which causes centripetal acceleration is called a centripetal force

explanations_tiered.definition: Centripetal force is the net force directed toward the center of a circular path that causes centripetal acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Without centripetal force, an object would move in a straight line; it is provided by tension, friction, gravity, or normal force.
explanations_tiered.beginner: When you swing a ball on a string, the string pulls it toward the center \u2013 that's centripetal force.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Centripetal force is always inward, perpendicular to velocity, causing direction change without speed change.
reasoning_steps: Force must point toward center to cause circular motion.
misconceptions: Centrifugal force is an apparent outward force in rotating frame, not a real force.
Centripetal force is not a new type of force; it is a role played by existing forces.

glossary_entry.definition: Net inward force causing circular motion.

flashcard.front: What is the direction of centripetal force?
flashcard.back: Toward the center of the circle.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Net force toward center causing circular motion.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify force required for circular motion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Centripetal force is directed toward the center.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: Which formula correctly gives centripetal force?
questions.hint: From Newton's second law: \(F = ma\) and \(a_c = v^2/r\).
questions.explanation: Centripetal force \(F_c = m a_c = m v^2 / r\). Option A is missing one power of v, giving incorrect units.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS

explanations_tiered.definition: Centripetal force equals mass times centripetal acceleration: \(F_c = m v^2 / r\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: This formula shows that for a given mass and radius, force increases with the square of speed.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The faster you swing something or the tighter the circle, the harder you have to pull inward.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From \(F = ma\) and \(a_c = v^2/r\), \(F_c = m v^2/r\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derived from Newton's second law applied to centripetal acceleration: \(\vec{F}_c = m \vec{a}_c = -\frac{mv^2}{r}\hat{r}\).
reasoning_steps: Start with Newton's second law: F = ma.

formula_analysis.name: Centripetal force formula
formula_analysis.derivation: From F = ma and a_c = v\u00b2/r

misconceptions: Using F = mv/r gives units of momentum, not force.
Forgetting to square v is a common error.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What is F_c in terms of m, v, r?
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify force required for circular motion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. F_c = m v\u00b2/r from F = ma and a_c = v\u00b2/r.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: A \(0.5 \ \mathrm{kg}\) ball is whirled in a horizontal circle of radius \(0.8 \ \mathrm{m}\) with speed \(4.0 \ \mathrm{m/s}\). What is the centripetal force?
questions.explanation: \(F_c = (0.5) \times (4.0)^2 / 0.8 = 0.5 \times 16 / 0.8 = 8 / 0.8 = 10 \ \mathrm{N}\). Option B is \(mv^2\) without dividing by r.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS

explanations_tiered.definition: Centripetal force is the inward force required to keep an object moving in a circle.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Given mass, speed, and radius, the centripetal force can be calculated directly from \(F_c = m v^2/r\).
explanations_tiered.beginner: To swing a 0.5 kg ball at 4 m/s in a 0.8 m circle, you need to pull with 10 N of force.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This force is provided by tension in the string; if the string breaks, the ball moves tangentially.
misconceptions: Using \(F = mv^2\) without dividing by r gives 8 N (option B).
Using \(F = mv/r\) gives 2.5 N (option A).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find centripetal force: m=0.5 kg, v=4 m/s, r=0.8 m.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Check order of operations: square v first.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.section: 3.2 Equations of Uniformly Accelerated Motion in 1D
questions.subsection: Determining the stopping distance of a vehicle
questions.question: What is the formula for braking distance when a vehicle decelerates due to friction?
questions.explanation: Braking distance comes from \(0 = v^2 + 2(-\mu g)s_b \Rightarrow s_b = v^2/(2\mu g)\). Option B omits the 2, giving half the actual distance.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.section_anchor: 3.2 Equations of Uniformly Accelerated Motion in 1D
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Determining the stopping distance of a vehicle

explanations_tiered.definition: Braking distance is the distance traveled while decelerating to a stop using friction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: It depends on initial speed squared, coefficient of friction, and gravity; higher \u03bc reduces stopping distance.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The faster you go, the longer it takes to stop; slippery roads (low \u03bc) also increase stopping distance.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using \(v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2as\) with \(v_f = 0\), \(a = -\mu g\) gives \(s = v^2/(2\mu g)\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: The deceleration \(a = -\mu g\) comes from friction \(F_f = \mu mg = ma\).

formula_analysis.name: Braking distance
formula_analysis.derivation: From kinematic equations and friction force

misconceptions: Omitting the 2 is common (option B).
Confusing braking distance with reaction distance.

glossary_entry.definition: Distance to stop after brakes applied: s_b = v\u00b2/(2\u03bcg).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Formula for braking distance using \u03bc and g?
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. s_b = v\u00b2/(2\u03bcg) from kinematics and friction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.section: 3.2 Equations of Uniformly Accelerated Motion in 1D
questions.subsection: Determining the stopping distance of a vehicle
questions.question: A car travels at \(20 \ \mathrm{m/s}\) on a dry road where \(\mu = 0.7\). What is the braking distance? (Use \(g = 9.8 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}\))
questions.explanation: \(s_b = (20)^2 / (2 \times 0.7 \times 9.8) = 400 / (13.72) \approx 29.2 \ \mathrm{m}\). Option A is \(v^2/(\mu g)\) without the 2, giving about 58.4/2? Actually 400/(0.7\u00d79.8)=58.4, which is option D.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.section_anchor: 3.2 Equations of Uniformly Accelerated Motion in 1D
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Determining the stopping distance of a vehicle

explanations_tiered.definition: Braking distance is the distance traveled while decelerating from initial speed to zero.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: On dry road (\u03bc=0.7), higher speed dramatically increases braking distance.
explanations_tiered.beginner: At 20 m/s (72 km/h) on dry road, you need about 29 meters to stop once brakes are applied.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This assumes constant deceleration \(a = -\mu g\); actual braking may vary but this is a standard approximation.
misconceptions: Forgetting the 2 gives 58.4 m (option D).
Using v not squared gives wrong units.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Show both dry and wet road comparisons.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.section: 3.2 Equations of Uniformly Accelerated Motion in 1D
questions.question: The equation \(s = \left(\frac{v_i + v_f}{2}\right)t\) is valid under what condition?

options.D: Only when initial velocity is zero

questions.hint: This equation uses average velocity = (v_i + v_f)/2, true only for constant acceleration.
questions.explanation: The formula \(s = v_{avg}t\) with \(v_{avg} = (v_i + v_f)/2\) is valid only when acceleration is constant. For non-constant acceleration, the average velocity is not simply the arithmetic mean.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.section_anchor: 3.2 Equations of Uniformly Accelerated Motion in 1D

explanations_tiered.definition: The formula \(s = \frac{v_i + v_f}{2} t\) holds only for constant (uniform) acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: When acceleration is constant, velocity changes linearly, so average velocity is the arithmetic mean of initial and final velocities.
explanations_tiered.beginner: This formula only works if the acceleration doesn't change during the motion.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: For constant a, v-t graph is linear, so area under graph (displacement) equals average height times width = ((v_i+v_f)/2)t.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Derived from \(s = v_i t + \frac12 a t^2\) and \(v_f = v_i + at\), eliminating a gives \(s = \frac{v_i+v_f}{2} t\).
reasoning_steps: Recall that average velocity = (v_i + v_f)/2 only for constant acceleration.
misconceptions: Using this formula for non-constant acceleration gives wrong displacement.
Assuming it always works.

publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Derive from area of trapezoid on v-t graph.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Derive and apply equations of motion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. It assumes linear velocity change (constant acceleration).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.section: 3.2 Equations of Uniformly Accelerated Motion in 1D
questions.question: A train slows uniformly from \(25 \ \mathrm{m/s}\) to \(15 \ \mathrm{m/s}\) over a distance of \(200 \ \mathrm{m}\). What is its acceleration?
questions.explanation: \(v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2as \Rightarrow 225 = 625 + 2a(200) \Rightarrow -400 = 400a \Rightarrow a = -1.0 \ \mathrm{m/s^2}\). Option A is half the correct magnitude.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.section_anchor: 3.2 Equations of Uniformly Accelerated Motion in 1D

explanations_tiered.definition: Acceleration is found from the time-independent kinematic equation.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Given initial and final velocities and displacement, acceleration can be calculated without time.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If a train slows down over a known distance, we can find how much it decelerates using \(v_f^2 = v_i^2 + 2as\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: Negative sign indicates deceleration opposite to direction of initial velocity.
misconceptions: Forgetting to square velocities.
Using \(v_f = v_i + at\) would require time, not given.

flashcard.front: v_i=25 m/s, v_f=15 m/s, s=200 m, find a.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Find a: v_i=25, v_f=15, s=200 m.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Solve problems of uniformly accelerated motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: A wheel of radius \(0.3 \ \mathrm{m}\) rotates with angular velocity \(20 \ \mathrm{rad/s}\). How does the tangential velocity of a point on the rim compare to that of a point halfway to the center?

options.A: The rim point has half the velocity
options.B: Both points have the same velocity
options.C: The rim point has twice the velocity
options.D: The rim point has four times the velocity

questions.hint: Tangential velocity \(v = \omega r\), with \(\omega\) constant for all points.
questions.explanation: Tangential velocity is proportional to radius: \(v = \omega r\). At the rim, \(r = 0.3 \ \mathrm{m}\); halfway, \(r = 0.15 \ \mathrm{m}\). Thus rim speed is twice that of halfway point.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.structural_element: Relationship between angular and tangential velocity
source_trace.content_anchor: angular velocity is the same for all points on the rotating object, while tangential velocity depends directly on its distance from the axis

explanations_tiered.definition: For a rigid rotating object, angular velocity \(\omega\) is constant, but tangential velocity \(v = \omega r\) increases with radius.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Points farther from the center move faster tangentially because they cover larger circumference in the same time.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The edge of a spinning wheel moves faster than a point near the hub.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Since \(v = \omega r\) and \(\omega\) is same, \(v \propto r\). Rim radius = 2 \u00d7 halfway radius, so rim speed is twice.
explanations_tiered.advanced: This is why the outer edge of a record moves faster than inner grooves; same angular speed, larger linear speed.
reasoning_steps: Compare radii: rim radius double halfway radius \u2192 speed double.
misconceptions: Thinking all points on a rotating object have same tangential speed (false; only same angular speed).
Believing \(v \propto 1/r\) instead of \(v \propto r\).

flashcard.front: Compare rim speed to halfway point speed on a wheel.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does rim tangential speed compare to halfway point?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: v_t = \u03c9r, so larger r gives larger v_t.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. v \u221d r; rim radius double gives double speed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.question: A student says: 'If centripetal acceleration is toward the center, why don't objects in circular motion move inward?' What is the correct explanation?

options.A: Centripetal acceleration is too small to change the path
options.B: Objects do move inward; that's why the path is curved
options.C: The object has tangential velocity that keeps it moving along the circle
options.D: Centripetal force is balanced by centrifugal force

questions.hint: Acceleration changes velocity direction, not just position.
questions.explanation: Centripetal acceleration continuously changes the direction of the tangential velocity, causing the object to turn but not move radially inward. Without tangential velocity, it would fall inward. Option D is wrong because centrifugal force is fictitious.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.content_anchor: centripetal acceleration changes direction of velocity

explanations_tiered.definition: Centripetal acceleration is perpendicular to velocity, changing only direction, not speed.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The object has tangential velocity; centripetal acceleration bends that velocity vector, causing circular path without radial motion.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You don't move inward because you're also moving sideways (tangentially), so you keep going around instead of falling in.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The velocity vector is tangent to the circle; centripetal acceleration acts perpendicular, turning the velocity without pulling the object to the center.
explanations_tiered.advanced: In polar coordinates, \(\vec{a} = (-r\dot\theta^2)\hat{r} + (\ddot{r})\hat{\theta}\); for uniform circular motion, \(\ddot{r}=0\) so only radial component changes direction.
reasoning_steps: Centripetal acceleration is perpendicular to velocity.
Perpendicular acceleration changes direction, not magnitude, keeping object on circle.
misconceptions: Thinking centripetal acceleration should pull object inward (it does, but tangential motion prevents radial collapse).
Believing centrifugal force balances centripetal (fictitious).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: a_c changes direction, not pulls inward.

glossary_entry.definition: Tangential velocity keeps object moving along circle; a_c only changes direction.

flashcard.front: Why don't objects fall inward despite centripetal acceleration?
flashcard.back: They have tangential velocity; a_c changes direction, not pulls radially inward.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Why circular motion doesn't go inward?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Tangential velocity keeps object moving along circle; a_c only changes direction.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Centripetal acceleration does not pull object to center because of tangential velocity.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: a_c changes direction, not pulls inward.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use vector diagram showing v and a_c perpendicular.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. Tangential velocity keeps object moving along the circle; a_c changes its direction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
questions.section: 3.2 Equations of Uniformly Accelerated Motion in 1D
questions.question: The equation \(s = v_i t + \frac12 a t^2\) fails if which condition occurs?
questions.hint: This equation is derived assuming constant acceleration.
questions.explanation: The equation \(s = v_i t + \frac12 a t^2\) is valid only for constant (uniform) acceleration. If acceleration changes with time, the relationship between s and t is more complex (requires integration).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: MOTION IN ONE AND TWO DIMENSIONS
source_trace.section_anchor: 3.2 Equations of Uniformly Accelerated Motion in 1D
source_trace.content_anchor: they do not apply if the acceleration is changing

explanations_tiered.definition: The kinematic equation \(s = v_i t + \frac12 a t^2\) assumes constant acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If acceleration varies, the displacement-time relationship is not quadratic with constant coefficient.
explanations_tiered.beginner: This formula only works if the acceleration doesn't change during the motion.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Derived from integrating \(a = \text{constant}\); if a varies, \(s = \int v\, dt\) with \(v = \int a(t) dt\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: For variable acceleration, \(s = v_i t + \int_0^t \int_0^{t'} a(t'') dt'' dt'\), which is not simply \(\frac12 a t^2\).
reasoning_steps: Recall derivation: a constant \u2192 v = v_i + at \u2192 s = \u222b v dt = v_i t + \u00bd a t\u00b2.
If a changes, the second integration is different.
misconceptions: Assuming it works for any motion.
Using it when acceleration is not constant gives wrong answer.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: s = v_i t + \u00bd a t\u00b2 only for constant a.

glossary_entry.definition: Fails if acceleration is not constant.

flashcard.front: When does s = v_i t + \u00bd a t\u00b2 fail?
flashcard.back: If acceleration changes with time (non-constant a).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Condition for s = v_i t + \u00bd a t\u00b2 to be invalid?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that real motion often has variable a, but we approximate.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Derive and apply equations of uniformly accelerated motion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. If acceleration changes, the equation no longer holds.
